title: Nomba One
text: Turn moving money once into running a subscription business: plans, cycles, proration, dunning, reconciliation, and settlement over every rail, built for how money actually moves in Nigeria. Nomba ships the primitives that move money. Nomba One is the layer that turns moving money once into running a subscription business (plans, cycles, proration, dunning, reconciliation, and settlement), so no team rebuilds it again. It runs over every rail (card, direct debit, transfer, crypto) and is built for how money actually moves here: thin balances, transfer first, where a failed charge usually means

title: Authentication
text: Every request authenticates with your organization's secret key, sent as a Bearer token. The key's prefix pins it to one mode: sandbox or live. The nombaone API authenticates with a single secret key per organization . You send it as a Bearer token on every request: There is no separate client id or signing step: the secret key both identifies your organization and proves the request is yours, so it must stay server side. Key format A secret key is a mode prefix followed by 256 bits of random body: Prefix Mode Example Sandbox Live The full secret is shown to you once , at creation, and is neve

text: The same authenticated request, in your language. Switch the tab: A missing, malformed, or revoked key returns . See for the fix. The error envelope:

title: Authentication — Key format
text: A secret key is a mode prefix followed by 256 bits of random body: Prefix Mode Example Sandbox Live The full secret is shown to you once , at creation, and is never recoverable afterward: the API stores only a hash. If you lose it, rotate the key and update your servers. The secret key can move money in live mode. Never commit it, never put it in client side code or a mobile bundle, and never log

title: Authentication — Rotating a key
text: Rotation issues a new secret and invalidates the old one. Because the key both authenticates and pins the mode, a rotated key keeps the same mode: a rotated sandbox key is still a sandbox key. Roll keys on a schedule and on any suspected exposure. Update every server that holds the old secret before the old key is revoked to avoid a gap.

title: Authentication — The key pins the mode
text: The mode is not a separate parameter: it is derived from the key's prefix and verified on every request. An key can only ever touch sandbox data; an key can only touch live data. There is no way for one request to cross the boundary, which is why you can keep both keys side by side without fear of a sandbox call reaching live. See environments for what that pinning means in practice.

title: Environments
text: Two independent axes: the deployment environment (development or production) is our infrastructure, and the account mode (sandbox or live) is set by your key's prefix and partitions your data. Most APIs jam two unrelated ideas into one "test/live" switch. nombaone keeps them separate, because they answer different questions: Deployment environment : where the code runs. Our infrastructure concern. Account mode : which data a request touches. Yours, chosen by your key. Axis Values Set by Do you see it? title: Verify us in your devtools
text: The sandbox sends no organic webhooks, so we hand you a real signed event: register an endpoint, fire it, and watch the signature verify in your own logs. Here is the honest truth most payment docs won't tell you: the Nomba sandbox does not push webhooks on its own. So instead of pretending, we hand you a real, signed event , byte for byte identical to production, that you fire on demand and watch land in your own tunnel. This page proves the pipe end to end: register → fire → verify. The event you fire below is a genuine signed payload from the sandbox instrument, the same shape and the same

title: Verify us in your devtools — 1 · Register an endpoint
text: Point a webhook at a URL you control. In development, expose your local server with a tunnel (ngrok, Cloudflare Tunnel) and register the public URL: The response includes the endpoint's signing secret ( ), shown once. Store it server side. You need it to verify every delivery.

title: Verify us in your devtools — 2 · Fire a real signed event
text: Ask the sandbox instrument to emit a signed event to your registered endpoint. Fire a happy path and an unhappy one: Watch your tunnel or your server logs: a real arrives at your endpoint, carrying the event body and the signature header. That is the exact traffic production sends.

title: Verify us in your devtools — 3 · Verify the signature
text: Never trust a webhook you haven't verified. Every delivery is signed over the raw request body. Verify before you parse. Compare in constant time. Compute the HMAC over the exact bytes you received, not over a re serialized object: then can reorder keys and change the bytes, and the signature will no longer match. Read the raw body first. Here is the verifier, running the real recipe in your brows

title: Verify us in your devtools — Then respond fast, and dedupe
text: Return quickly and do the work asynchronously. A slow handler looks like a failure and gets retried. Because delivery is at least once, dedupe on the event id : the same event may arrive twice, and retrying the webhook is not retrying the charge. The full receive → verify → dedupe → act pattern for one correct balance. Every event type, when it fires, and its payload shape.

title: Coupons and credits
text: Two ways to reduce what a customer pays: a coupon discounts an invoice by a percentage or a fixed amount, a credit grant is a balance that draws down first. There are two distinct tools for charging a customer less, and they resolve differently on an invoice. A coupon is a discount rule (percent or fixed) you attach to a customer or subscription. A credit is a stored balance that draws down before any charge. Both land in the ledger as explicit lines, so an invoice always shows exactly why the total is what it is. title: Coupons and credits — Coupon vs credit: which to use
text: Coupon : a rule that reduces the price (a promo, a discounted tier). It recomputes each cycle for its duration. Credit : a balance that is spent once, then gone. Use it for refunds you want kept on platform, or prepaid top ups. On one invoice, credits and coupons both apply: the discount reduces the amount due, then credit draws down the remainder. title: Coupons and credits — Grant a credit
text: A credit is money on the customer's account that draws down before any charge, useful for refunds as credit, goodwill, or prepaid balances: When an invoice is collected, available credit is applied before the rail is charged, oldest grant first. The invoice shows the credit line and the reduced amount actually charged, every kobo accounted for in the ledger.

title: Dunning and recovery
text: Recover a failed charge on a thin balance the Nigerian way: retries tuned for "not yet," a card OTP checkout link, and the events that tell you when to restore access. When a charge fails, most billing systems assume "no." In Nigeria the honest read is usually "not yet" : the balance is thin and will top up, or the card needs a one time OTP. Dunning is the engine's recovery loop for exactly this, and it is tuned for recovery, not just blind retries. See dunning for thin balances for the reasoning; this guide is how you operate it. title: Handle webhooks
text: Receive events, verify the signature, dedupe on the event id, and act: the four step pattern that keeps one correct balance no matter how many times an event arrives. Webhooks are how nombaone tells your server what happened: an invoice was paid, a charge failed, a card needs OTP, a transfer arrived. Handling them correctly is four steps ( receive, verify, dedupe, act ), and getting any one wrong is how balances drift. This guide is the pattern; to see events land in your own logs first, do verify us in your devtools. 1 · Register an endpoint The response returns a signing secret ( ), shown on

title: Handle webhooks — 1 · Register an endpoint
text: The response returns a signing secret ( ), shown once. Store it server side. Register endpoints per mode: a sandbox event only reaches your sandbox endpoint.

title: Handle webhooks — 2 · Verify the signature: on the raw body
text: Every delivery carries a signature over . Verify before you parse : compute the HMAC over the exact bytes received, and compare in constant time. Read the raw request body and HMAC those exact bytes. then can reorder keys, changing the bytes so the signature no longer matches. Frameworks that auto parse the body will break verification. Capture the raw body first.

title: Handle webhooks — 3 · Dedupe on the event id
text: Delivery is at least once : the same event can arrive more than once (a retry, a network hiccup). Record each you've processed and skip repeats. The handler must be idempotent, because retrying the webhook is not retrying the charge: reacting twice to one must not credit a balance twice.

title: Handle webhooks — 4 · Respond fast, act async
text: Return immediately, then do the work. A slow handler reads as a failure and gets retried, which is fine (you dedupe), but keeping the response fast avoids pile ups.

title: Handle webhooks — The events that matter
text: Handle the unhappy paths, not just : : a cycle collected. Grant access, extend the period. : a charge failed; the subscription is and dunning is retrying. Don't cancel access yet: on a thin balance this is "not yet." : a card needs OTP; the payload carries a fresh checkout link to send the customer. : dunning (or the customer completing OTP) succeeded; restore normal state. title: Proration and plan changes
text: Upgrade, downgrade, or switch intervals mid cycle: one call, with proration computed as balanced ledger legs so the customer is charged exactly the difference. A customer rarely stays on one price forever. They upgrade, downgrade, add seats, or switch from monthly to yearly, usually mid cycle , when they've already paid for part of the period. A plan change moves the subscription to a new price and settles the difference, and because proration is a ledger problem it's computed in balanced kobo legs, not floating point guesses. One call handles the switch and the proration: The engine credits the unused portion of the old price and charges the used portion of the new one, to the exact kobo. On an upgrade the customer pays the difference now; on a downgrade the credit applies to the next invoice.

title: Refunds, payouts & settlement
text: Move money back out: refund a settled charge (the tenant leg only), read the escrow lock, and pay an organization's net to a bank account. When a charge is collected and verified, the money splits at collection : your platform fee and the organization's net, each posted as balanced ledger legs. This guide is how money moves back out: refunds, the escrow lock that guards them, and payouts. The model is explained in settlement & sub accounts; here is how to operate it. Refund a settlement A refund reverses a settled charge back toward the payer. By default it refunds the full net; pass for a par

title: Refunds, payouts & settlement — Pay out to a bank account
text: Withdraw an organization's available balance to a bank: Two guards protect the money: : you tried to withdraw funds still inside the escrow window. Wait for release, or withdraw only the available portion. : the amount is more than the available balance. A payout posts the ledger debit before the bank transfer is attempted, and if the transfer fails it posts a compensating reversal. Funds are neve

title: Refunds, payouts & settlement — Read the escrow lock
text: Settled funds are not instantly withdrawable. A recent settlement escrow lock holds them for a window so a refund can be clawed back before the money leaves. Check what's locked vs available: The response shows the locked amount and when it releases. Available balance for payout is the settled net minus the escrow lock (and any minimum buffer).

title: Refunds, payouts & settlement — Refund a settlement
text: A refund reverses a settled charge back toward the payer. By default it refunds the full net; pass for a partial: A refund reverses only the organization's leg : it debits the organization's settlement balance and credits back the payer. The platform fee is non refundable and is never touched. This keeps the ledger honest: a refund posts a new reversing transaction, it does not mutate the original

What differs is the payment method you attach, because rails collect differently: card and mandate are pulled , a bank transfer is pushed . Everything Nomba One does is in service of one loop: open a cycle, produce an invoice, collect it, and record the truth in the ledger , then do it again next period. title: How billing works — What makes this hard here
text: Billing engines from elsewhere assume a card on file and a full balance. Neither holds in Nigeria: money arrives by transfer, balances are thin, and a failed charge usually means "not yet," not "no." The rest of these concept pages are about the parts that follow from that reality: the ledger, integer kobo, multi rail push/pull, and settlement.

title: Money is integer kobo
text: Why every amount in the API is an integer in kobo, how the naira boundary works, and how the 100× overcharge trap is designed out. Every amount in the Nomba One API is an integer in kobo . There are no floats, no decimal strings, and no ambiguity: , so is ₦2,500.00. Why kobo, and why integers Floating point money is a correctness bug waiting to happen: is not , and rounding errors accumulate across a subscription's lifetime. Integer minor units make arithmetic exact and auditable. The ledger stores integer kobo, the API speaks integer kobo, and the two can never disagree. Every money field say

title: Money is integer kobo — Every money field says its unit
text: To make the unit impossible to mistake, every money field name ends in : , , , , and so on. If a field carries money, its name tells you the unit. You never have to guess.

title: Money is integer kobo — Rounding
text: Proration and percentage discounts can produce fractional kobo. Nomba One resolves these in the ledger with a fixed, documented order so the legs always balance to the exact kobo. See proration is a ledger problem.

title: Money is integer kobo — The naira boundary
text: Here is the one place the unit changes, and we handle it so you never have to. Nomba's own checkout surface reports amounts in naira , while our engine and ledger are integer kobo . If those two were ever confused, a charge would be off by 100×. Nomba One converts only at the Nomba boundary (kobo → naira on the way out to Nomba, naira → kobo on the way back) and never anywhere else. Inside your in

text: Floating point money is a correctness bug waiting to happen: is not , and rounding errors accumulate across a subscription's lifetime. Integer minor units make arithmetic exact and auditable. The ledger stores integer kobo, the API speaks integer kobo, and the two can never disagree.

title: Multi-rail: push and pull
text: Why card and mandate rails are pulled but bank transfer is pushed, and how one subscription runs correctly over all of them. Nomba One runs one subscription over every rail: card, direct debit, bank transfer, and crypto. But the rails are not interchangeable, and pretending they are is the most common way to get billing wrong here. The dividing line is push vs pull . Pull rails: we initiate A pull rail lets the engine initiate the money movement on the billing date: Card : charge a saved card token. In Nigeria a recurring card charge often hits a bank OTP/3 D Secure step up, so it is not alway

title: Multi-rail: push and pull — Pull rails: we initiate
text: A pull rail lets the engine initiate the money movement on the billing date: Card : charge a saved card token. In Nigeria a recurring card charge often hits a bank OTP/3 D Secure step up, so it is not always silent. See card tokens expire and the OTP recovery model. Direct debit (mandate) : pull from a bank account the customer has authorized with a mandate. Reliable and silent once active. For a

title: Multi-rail: push and pull — Push rails: the customer initiates
text: A push rail cannot be pulled. There is no API call that reaches into a customer's account and takes the money. The customer has to send it : Bank transfer : the most common way money moves in Nigeria. On the billing date the engine exposes where to pay (a virtual account) and then waits for the money to arrive. For a push rail, "collect" means: expose the pay instructions, and settle when a verifi

title: Multi-rail: push and pull — Why the asymmetry shapes everything
text: Because a transfer can't be pulled, a "charge on the billing date and mark it paid" model simply has nowhere to fit it. Nomba One is built around the asymmetry instead of hiding it: push rails settle asynchronously, thin balances mean a failed pull is "not yet" not "no" , and dunning is tuned for recovery, not just retries.

title: Settlement & sub-accounts
text: How a verified collection splits into the platform fee and the merchant's net, settles to their Nomba sub account, and stays claw back safe with an escrow lock. When a subscription charge is collected and verified, the money doesn't just sit in one pool. It splits at collection and settles to the right place. Nomba One is multi tenant by design: each organization's money is its own. Split at collection A verified collection is split into two legs, recorded in the ledger: The platform fee : the fee your platform charges, computed by the fee engine. The organization's net : what's left, which be

title: Settlement & sub-accounts — Escrow, refunds, and payouts
text: Settled funds are not instantly withdrawable. A recent settlement escrow lock holds them for a window so a refund can be clawed back before the money leaves: Refund : reverses only the organization's leg back toward the payer; the platform fee is non refundable and is never touched. A refund posts a new reversing transaction, so the ledger stays complete. Payout : withdraws the organization's avai

title: Settlement & sub-accounts — Split at collection
text: A verified collection is split into two legs, recorded in the ledger: The platform fee : the fee your platform charges, computed by the fee engine. The organization's net : what's left, which belongs to the merchant. The net settles to that organization's Nomba sub account . The split is posted as balanced ledger legs, so gross always equals fee plus net, to the exact kobo.

title: Settlement & sub-accounts — Sub-accounts
text: A sub account is the organization's account on Nomba where its settled net lands. Multi tenancy means one integration can run many merchants, each with their own sub account, their own customers, and their own isolated money. See isolation is a data model property.

title: The double-entry ledger
text: The source of truth beneath every invoice, charge, refund, and settlement: how double entry recording keeps the money provably correct. Underneath every subscription, invoice, and payout is a double entry ledger . It is the source of truth: not the invoice status, not a cached balance, not a webhook. The ledger. Every other number the API returns is derived from it. Debits and credits, not signs Each money movement is recorded as a transaction made of balanced legs . Every leg is a positive integer kobo amount with a direction (a debit or a credit) against an account . The direction, not a plu

title: The double-entry ledger — Debits and credits, not signs
text: Each money movement is recorded as a transaction made of balanced legs . Every leg is a positive integer kobo amount with a direction (a debit or a credit) against an account . The direction, not a plus or minus sign, carries the meaning. A transaction is only valid if its debits equal its credits: the books always balance, by construction. For example, a paid ₦5,000 invoice posts a transaction th

title: The double-entry ledger — Idempotency lives here too
text: Because the ledger is the source of truth, idempotency is enforced against it: a money movement claims its effect atomically, so a retried or replayed request resolves to exactly one posting. This is how "retrying the webhook is not retrying the charge" stays true: the ledger only ever records the movement once. Send the same twice and you get one subscription back; change the key and you get a ne

title: The double-entry ledger — Why this matters
text: Nothing silently drifts. A cached "balance" can be wrong; a ledger that must balance cannot hide a missing kobo. Balances are read from the ledger, not stored beside it. Every movement is auditable. A refund, a credit grant, a settlement, a payout: each is a transaction you can trace, with legs that name exactly what moved and where. Reversals are first class. title: Dunning for thin balances: why payday-timed retries beat fixed schedules

On the 1st, the charge fails: insufficient funds. Their salary lands on the 28th. Whether you keep this customer comes down to one thing: which day you retry. The naive approach Retry on a fixed ladder: try again in a day, then three days, then a week. It is simple, it is what most billing libraries ship, and it treats every failure the same. Retry a thin balance on the 2nd, the 5th, and the 8th, and you have spent your whole retry budget days before payday, on an account you already know is empty. In Nigeria most subscription churn is involuntary: the customer never chose to leave, your schedule just gave up before their salary arrived.

Once active, it is the most reliable silent rail in Nigeria: no OTP per charge, no thin balance card decline. See mandates and consent for the lifecycle (create → consent → active → debit). A mandate created today is until the bank confirms it. The engine sweeps pending mandates to and fires when one activates, you don't poll.

title: Metrics
text: Billing metrics: MRR, churn, and the dunning funnel, computed from the ledger. Generated from the live schema. Metrics are the health of your billing, computed from the ledger rather than estimated: monthly recurring revenue, churn, and the dunning recovery funnel. Because they read the same source of truth as every charge and settlement, they can't drift from the money. Metrics are computed on read from ledger truth, so a cached number can never quietly disagree with reality.

title: Delivery guarantee
text: Delivery is at least once, never exactly once. The same event can arrive twice. Dedupe on the event id and make every handler idempotent. nombaone guarantees at least once delivery, and says so on every delivery in the header. That is a deliberate, honest choice: exactly once delivery over an unreliable network is not achievable, and any API that claims it is lying. What we give you instead is a guarantee you can build on, plus the one tool that makes it safe: a stable event id to dedupe on. What at least once means for you The same event can arrive more than once: a retry after a slow respons

title: Delivery guarantee — Retries and ordering
text: Retries. A delivery that doesn't get a timely is retried on a backoff schedule. Return quickly and do the work async so a slow handler doesn't trigger avoidable retries. Ordering is not guaranteed. Events can arrive out of order. Don't assume lands before . Reconcile against the resource ( ) when order matters, rather than inferring state from event sequence. Replay. You can re request a past deli

title: Delivery guarantee — What at-least-once means for you
text: The same event can arrive more than once: a retry after a slow response, a network hiccup, a redelivery. Your handler must treat a repeat as a no op. Record every you've processed and skip repeats. Do this after signature verification, so an unverified body can never poison your dedupe store.

title: Delivery guarantee — Why idempotency has to go all the way down
text: Deduping at the edge is not enough on its own: the work the handler triggers must itself be idempotent, because retrying the webhook is not retrying the charge. If a duplicate slips through, crediting a balance twice is a real bug. The ledger enforces this at the bottom: a money movement is claimed once, so even a double processed event resolves to one posting.

title: Retries & replay
text: A delivery that doesn't get a timely 2xx is retried on a backoff schedule; you can also replay any past delivery by hand. Both preserve the event id. Networks fail, servers restart, deploys happen mid request. nombaone assumes your endpoint will occasionally miss a delivery and is built to recover: automatically with retries, and manually with replay. Both reuse the same event id , so a correctly deduped handler treats a recovered delivery as already seen. Automatic retries A delivery succeeds when your endpoint returns a promptly. Anything else (a timeout, a , a connection error) is retried o

title: Retries & replay — Automatic retries
text: A delivery succeeds when your endpoint returns a promptly. Anything else (a timeout, a , a connection error) is retried on an increasing backoff, giving a briefly down endpoint time to come back without you doing anything. The fastest way to avoid retries is to acknowledge immediately and process asynchronously. A handler that does slow work inline looks like a failure and earns retries it didn't

title: Retries & replay — Missed a window? Reconcile, don't reconstruct
text: If your endpoint was down and you're unsure what you missed, don't try to rebuild state from a guessed event sequence. Read the resource directly ( ) or replay the specific deliveries. title: Retries & replay — Replay a delivery
text: Redeliver a past event on demand, after fixing a handler bug, or to backfill an endpoint that was down: A replay is the same event, delivered again, not a new one. Your dedupe store should recognize it as already processed. That is exactly what you want: replay is safe precisely because your handler is idempotent.

title: Signing & verification
text: Every delivery is signed HMAC SHA256 over the timestamp and the raw body. Verify before you parse, compare in constant time, and reject stale timestamps. Never trust a webhook you haven't verified: the URL is public, so anyone could to it. Every genuine delivery carries an header you check against your endpoint's signing secret. The signature The header is , where is: is the delivery timestamp and is the exact bytes of the request body. Verify by recomputing and comparing. Compute the HMAC over the body exactly as received. then can reorder keys and change the bytes, so the signature won't mat

title: Signing & verification — The signature
text: The header is , where is: is the delivery timestamp and is the exact bytes of the request body. Verify by recomputing and comparing. Compute the HMAC over the body exactly as received. then can reorder keys and change the bytes, so the signature won't match. Capture the raw body before any framework parses it.

title: Signing & verification — Two rules that matter
text: Constant time compare. Use / , never . A plain compare leaks the secret one byte at a time under timing attack. Reject stale timestamps. Bound to a few minutes so a captured delivery can't be replayed against you later.

title: Sandbox toolkit overview

title: Error: CLIENT_INVALID_REQUEST
text: CLIENT_INVALID_REQUEST: The request could not be understood. Check the method, path, headers, and that the JSON body is well-formed before retrying.

title: Error: CLIENT_VALIDATION_FAILED
text: CLIENT_VALIDATION_FAILED: One or more fields are invalid. Read the `fields` map in this response — each key is a field path and its messages tell you exactly what to fix.

title: Error: CLIENT_FORBIDDEN
text: CLIENT_FORBIDDEN: Your key is valid but not allowed to perform this action. Confirm the resource belongs to your organization and that your key has the required scope.

title: Error: CLIENT_ROUTE_NOT_FOUND
text: CLIENT_ROUTE_NOT_FOUND: No route matches this method and path. Check the HTTP verb and the URL (including the `/v1` version prefix) against the API reference.

title: Error: CLIENT_RESOURCE_NOT_FOUND
text: CLIENT_RESOURCE_NOT_FOUND: No resource exists at that id in this mode. Verify the id and confirm your key targets the same mode (sandbox vs live) the resource lives in.

title: Error: CLIENT_CONFLICT
text: CLIENT_CONFLICT: This request conflicts with the current state of the resource. Re-fetch it to see its latest state, then retry with values consistent with that state.

title: Error: INVALID_CURSOR
text: INVALID_CURSOR: The pagination `cursor` is malformed or expired. Drop the `cursor` to start from the first page, then page forward using only the `nextCursor` values we return.

title: Error: API_KEY_MISSING
text: API_KEY_MISSING: Send your secret key as `Authorization: Bearer <key>`. Create one in the dashboard under API keys if you do not have it.

title: Error: API_KEY_INVALID
text: API_KEY_INVALID: That key is not recognized. Copy it fresh from the dashboard (it may have been rotated or revoked) and send the whole `nbo_sandbox_`/`nbo_live_` string with no extra whitespace.

title: Error: API_KEY_SCOPE_FORBIDDEN
text: API_KEY_SCOPE_FORBIDDEN: Your key lacks the scope this endpoint needs. Grant the required scope (e.g. `customers:write`) to the key in the dashboard, or use a key that already has it.

title: Error: API_KEY_ENVIRONMENT_MISMATCH
text: API_KEY_ENVIRONMENT_MISMATCH: A `nbo_live_` key is only accepted on the production deployment. Use your `nbo_sandbox_` key to build against sandbox, and switch to the `nbo_live_` key once you go live in production.

title: Error: API_KEY_HOST_MISMATCH
text: API_KEY_HOST_MISMATCH: Your key's mode does not match the host you called. Send `nbo_sandbox_` keys to the sandbox host and `nbo_live_` keys to the live host — the base URL and the key prefix must agree.

title: Error: IDEMPOTENCY_KEY_MISSING
text: IDEMPOTENCY_KEY_MISSING: Send a unique `Idempotency-Key` header on this request; reuse the same key to safely retry the same operation.

title: Error: IDEMPOTENCY_KEY_REUSED
text: IDEMPOTENCY_KEY_REUSED: You reused an `Idempotency-Key` with a different request body. Use a new key for a new operation, or resend the identical body to replay the original result.

title: Error: IDEMPOTENCY_IN_PROGRESS
text: IDEMPOTENCY_IN_PROGRESS: A request with this `Idempotency-Key` is still being processed. Wait briefly and retry with the same key to get the final result.

title: Error: RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED
text: RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED: You're sending requests too quickly. Back off and retry after the interval in the `Retry-After` header.

title: Error: PLATFORM_MAINTENANCE
text: PLATFORM_MAINTENANCE: The platform is briefly paused for maintenance and is rejecting writes. Reads still work; retry your write after a short wait.

title: Error: WEBHOOK_SIGNATURE_INVALID
text: WEBHOOK_SIGNATURE_INVALID: The webhook signature did not verify. Confirm you are using the correct signing secret for this endpoint and verifying against the exact raw request body (no re-serialization).

title: Error: CUSTOMER_NOT_FOUND
text: CUSTOMER_NOT_FOUND: No customer exists with that id in this mode. Check the id, and confirm you are using the key for the same mode (sandbox vs live) the customer was created in.

title: Error: CUSTOMER_EMAIL_TAKEN
text: CUSTOMER_EMAIL_TAKEN: A customer with this email already exists in your organization. Reuse the existing customer, or create this one with a different email.

title: Error: PLAN_NOT_FOUND
text: PLAN_NOT_FOUND: No plan exists with that id in this environment. Verify the plan id, and that your key matches the environment the plan was created in.

title: Error: PLAN_NAME_TAKEN
text: PLAN_NAME_TAKEN: A plan with this name already exists in your organization. Pick a different name, or update the existing plan instead of creating a new one.

title: Error: PLAN_ALREADY_ARCHIVED
text: PLAN_ALREADY_ARCHIVED: This plan is already archived, so it cannot be archived or modified again. Unarchive it first if you need to change it.

title: Error: PLAN_HAS_ACTIVE_SUBSCRIBERS
text: PLAN_HAS_ACTIVE_SUBSCRIBERS: You cannot archive or delete a plan that still has active subscriptions. Migrate or cancel those subscriptions first, then retry.

title: Error: PRICE_NOT_FOUND
text: PRICE_NOT_FOUND: No price exists with that id in this environment. Check the price id, and that your key matches the environment it was created in.

title: Error: PRICE_PLAN_MISMATCH
text: PRICE_PLAN_MISMATCH: That price does not belong to the plan you referenced. Use a price that belongs to this plan, or reference the plan the price actually belongs to.

title: Error: PRICE_ALREADY_INACTIVE
text: PRICE_ALREADY_INACTIVE: This price is already inactive. No action is needed; create a new active price if you need one to subscribe against.

title: Error: PRICE_TIERED_NOT_SUPPORTED
text: PRICE_TIERED_NOT_SUPPORTED: Tiered/graduated pricing is not supported here. Use a flat per-unit price for this operation.

title: Error: PAYMENT_METHOD_NOT_FOUND
text: PAYMENT_METHOD_NOT_FOUND: No payment method exists with that id for this customer. Verify the id, and that it belongs to the customer and environment you are calling with.

title: Error: PAYMENT_METHOD_NOT_ACTIVE
text: PAYMENT_METHOD_NOT_ACTIVE: This payment method is not active (it may be pending, expired, or detached). Have the customer re-authorize or add a new payment method before charging.

title: Error: PAYMENT_METHOD_KIND_MISMATCH
text: PAYMENT_METHOD_KIND_MISMATCH: This operation needs a different kind of payment method than the one provided (e.g. a card where a bank mandate was given). Supply a payment method of the required kind.

title: Error: MANDATE_NOT_ACTIVE
text: MANDATE_NOT_ACTIVE: The mandate is not active yet (or has been revoked), so it cannot be charged. Wait for the customer to authorize it, or start a new mandate.

title: Error: MANDATE_MAX_AMOUNT_EXCEEDED
text: MANDATE_MAX_AMOUNT_EXCEEDED: The charge is larger than the mandate's authorized maximum. Lower the amount, or have the customer authorize a new mandate with a higher limit.

title: Error: MANDATE_CONSENT_PENDING
text: MANDATE_CONSENT_PENDING: The customer has not finished authorizing this mandate. Send them back through the consent link and wait for the activation webhook before charging.

title: Error: SUBSCRIPTION_NOT_FOUND
text: SUBSCRIPTION_NOT_FOUND: No subscription exists with that id in this environment. Check the id, and that your key matches the environment it was created in.

title: Error: SUBSCRIPTION_ILLEGAL_TRANSITION
text: SUBSCRIPTION_ILLEGAL_TRANSITION: The subscription cannot move to that state from its current one (e.g. resuming a canceled subscription). Re-fetch it to see its current status and choose a valid action.

title: Error: SUBSCRIPTION_VERSION_CONFLICT
text: SUBSCRIPTION_VERSION_CONFLICT: The subscription changed since you loaded it (optimistic-concurrency conflict). Re-fetch it, reapply your change on the latest version, and retry.

title: Error: SUBSCRIPTION_NOT_TERMINAL
text: SUBSCRIPTION_NOT_TERMINAL: This action requires the subscription to be in a terminal state (e.g. canceled). Cancel or end it first, then retry.

title: Error: SUBSCRIPTION_PAYMENT_METHOD_REQUIRED
text: SUBSCRIPTION_PAYMENT_METHOD_REQUIRED: This subscription needs a usable payment method before it can be billed. Attach an active card or authorized mandate to the customer, then retry.

title: Error: INVOICE_NOT_FOUND
text: INVOICE_NOT_FOUND: No invoice exists with that id in this environment. Verify the id, and that your key matches the environment it was created in.

title: Error: INVOICE_ALREADY_FINALIZED
text: INVOICE_ALREADY_FINALIZED: This invoice is already finalized and its line items are locked. Issue a credit note or a new invoice instead of editing this one.

title: Error: INVOICE_ALREADY_PAID
text: INVOICE_ALREADY_PAID: This invoice is already paid, so it cannot be paid or voided again. If you need to reverse it, issue a refund against the payment.

title: Error: INVOICE_NOT_VOIDABLE
text: INVOICE_NOT_VOIDABLE: Only open, unpaid invoices can be voided. Because this invoice is paid or already void, issue a refund or credit note instead.

title: Error: SUBSCRIPTION_SCHEDULE_NOT_FOUND
text: SUBSCRIPTION_SCHEDULE_NOT_FOUND: No subscription schedule exists with that id. Check the id, or list schedules on the subscription to find the correct one.

title: Error: SUBSCRIPTION_SCHEDULE_CONFLICT
text: SUBSCRIPTION_SCHEDULE_CONFLICT: This schedule overlaps or conflicts with an existing one on the subscription. Cancel or adjust the conflicting schedule, then retry.

title: Error: SUBSCRIPTION_SCHEDULE_INVALID_EFFECTIVE_AT
text: SUBSCRIPTION_SCHEDULE_INVALID_EFFECTIVE_AT: The schedule's `effectiveAt` is invalid — it must be in the future and align with the billing cycle. Choose a valid future timestamp and retry.

title: Error: PRORATION_NOT_APPLICABLE
text: PRORATION_NOT_APPLICABLE: Proration does not apply to this change (for example, no mid-cycle difference to prorate). Proceed without proration, or verify the change you intended.

title: Error: PRORATION_INTERVAL_SWITCH_UNSUPPORTED
text: PRORATION_INTERVAL_SWITCH_UNSUPPORTED: Prorating across a change of billing interval (e.g. monthly to yearly) is not supported. Schedule the interval change at the next period boundary instead.

title: Error: COUPON_NOT_FOUND
text: COUPON_NOT_FOUND: No coupon matches that id or code in this environment. Check the code for typos and that your key matches the environment the coupon was created in.

title: Error: COUPON_EXPIRED
text: COUPON_EXPIRED: This coupon is past its redeem-by date and can no longer be applied. Use a currently valid coupon, or issue a new one.

title: Error: COUPON_MAX_REDEMPTIONS_REACHED
text: COUPON_MAX_REDEMPTIONS_REACHED: This coupon has hit its maximum number of redemptions. Raise its redemption limit, or create a new coupon for further use.

title: Error: COUPON_INVALID_DEFINITION
text: COUPON_INVALID_DEFINITION: The coupon's definition is inconsistent (e.g. both a percent and a fixed amount, or a non-positive value). Fix the definition so exactly one valid discount is specified.

title: Error: COUPON_ALREADY_APPLIED
text: COUPON_ALREADY_APPLIED: This coupon is already applied to the subscription or invoice. Remove the existing discount first if you intend to reapply or change it.

title: Error: DISCOUNT_NOT_FOUND
text: DISCOUNT_NOT_FOUND: No discount exists with that id on this subscription or invoice. Verify the id, or list discounts to find the correct one.

title: Error: CREDIT_GRANT_NOT_FOUND
text: CREDIT_GRANT_NOT_FOUND: No credit grant exists with that id for this customer. Check the id, and that it belongs to the customer and environment you are calling with.

title: Error: CREDIT_GRANT_ALREADY_VOIDED
text: CREDIT_GRANT_ALREADY_VOIDED: This credit grant is already voided and cannot be voided or spent again. Issue a new credit grant if the customer needs more credit.

title: Error: CREDIT_INSUFFICIENT_BALANCE
text: CREDIT_INSUFFICIENT_BALANCE: The customer's credit balance is too low to cover this amount. Grant more credit, or apply a smaller amount.

title: Error: CREDIT_INVALID_AMOUNT
text: CREDIT_INVALID_AMOUNT: The credit amount is invalid — it must be a positive integer in the smallest currency unit. Send a valid amount and retry.

title: Error: DUNNING_NO_OPEN_INVOICE
text: DUNNING_NO_OPEN_INVOICE: There is no open, past-due invoice to run dunning against. Confirm the subscription actually has an unpaid invoice before triggering recovery.

title: Error: DUNNING_ATTEMPT_NOT_FOUND
text: DUNNING_ATTEMPT_NOT_FOUND: No dunning attempt exists with that id. Verify the id, or list the attempts on the invoice to find the correct one.

title: Error: DUNNING_CARD_UPDATE_REQUIRED
text: DUNNING_CARD_UPDATE_REQUIRED: The card needs customer action (such as OTP/3DS) that cannot be completed headlessly. Send the customer the hosted payment/checkout link to update or re-authorize their card.

title: Error: DUNNING_ALREADY_TERMINAL
text: DUNNING_ALREADY_TERMINAL: This dunning process has already ended (recovered or exhausted) and cannot be advanced. Start a new billing attempt if the invoice is still unpaid.

title: Error: SETTLEMENT_NOT_FOUND
text: SETTLEMENT_NOT_FOUND: No settlement exists with that id in this environment. Verify the id, and that your key matches the environment it belongs to.

title: Error: SETTLEMENT_SUBACCOUNT_NOT_FOUND
text: SETTLEMENT_SUBACCOUNT_NOT_FOUND: No settlement subaccount is configured for this organization. Create and verify the subaccount before recording splits or payouts.

title: Error: REFUND_ALREADY_REFUNDED
text: REFUND_ALREADY_REFUNDED: This charge has already been fully refunded. Inspect the existing refund; there is nothing left to refund.

title: Error: REFUND_AMOUNT_EXCEEDS_NET
text: REFUND_AMOUNT_EXCEEDS_NET: The refund is larger than the remaining refundable (net) amount on the charge. Refund at most the remaining balance shown on the charge.

title: Error: ESCROW_LOCKED
text: ESCROW_LOCKED: These funds are held in escrow and cannot be paid out or refunded yet. Wait for the escrow hold to release, then retry.

title: Error: PAYOUT_EXCEEDS_AVAILABLE
text: PAYOUT_EXCEEDS_AVAILABLE: The payout is larger than the available (non-escrowed, settled) balance. Reduce the amount to at most the available balance, or wait for more funds to settle.

title: Error: QUOTA_EXCEEDED
text: QUOTA_EXCEEDED: You've reached a plan or account quota for this operation. Back off and retry later, or contact support to raise the quota.

title: Error: EXAMPLE_NOT_FOUND
text: EXAMPLE_NOT_FOUND: No example resource exists with that id in this environment. Check the id, and that your key matches the environment it was created in.

title: Error: SYSTEM_INTERNAL_ERROR
text: SYSTEM_INTERNAL_ERROR: Something failed on our side. Retry shortly; if it persists, contact support with the `requestId` from the response `meta`.

title: Error: SYSTEM_UPSTREAM_ERROR
text: SYSTEM_UPSTREAM_ERROR: An upstream dependency was unavailable or timed out. This is transient — retry after a short backoff; if it persists, contact support with the `requestId`.
